items.q: What is the difference between batch and mass production?
items.correct: Batch makes a set quantity of one product then switches; mass production makes large quantities continuously to one specification.
items.misconception: They are just two names for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Batch production only happens at night.
items.why: Students confuse the limited, switchable runs of batch production with the high-volume standardised flow of mass production.

items.q: Why is continuous production suited to products like bottled drinks?
items.correct: Because demand is high and constant, so the line runs without stopping to produce huge volumes cheaply.
items.misconception: Because the products are expensive and made one at a time.
items.random_wrong: Because continuous production needs no machinery.
items.why: Learners mismatch continuous flow with low-volume bespoke work rather than high, steady demand.

items.q: What does 'throughput' measure in food manufacturing?
items.correct: The amount of product passing through the production process in a given time.
items.misconception: The total cost of the factory building.
items.why: Students confuse a measure of output rate with unrelated measures of cost or staffing.

items.q: Continuous production is best suited to:
items.why: Correct answer: 24/7 stable-demand products. “Wedding cakes” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Defined quantity of identical units. “One unit” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a continuous line runs 24 hours producing 600 units/hour, daily output (units) is:
items.why: The real figure is 14400. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
